---
title: "Import Data"
---

```{r, include=FALSE}
source("../src/stata_machine.R")
library(Statamarkdown)
stataexe <- get_stata_machine_path()
knitr::opts_chunk$set(engine.path=list(stata=stataexe))
```

```{stata, echo = FALSE, results = "hide", collectcode = TRUE}
do "../src/lissyuse.do"
```

More than a thousand LIS and LWS datasets are available for use and analysis within LISSY. Learning how to import these datasets is one of the first essential steps in the user workflow, as it requires becoming familiar with the built-in tools specifically designed for this purpose.

Before getting started, there are a few important concepts to keep in mind:

- LIS and LWS datasets cannot be accessed simultaneously (remember to select the appropriate **Project** from the dropdown menu in the user interface).

- Datasets are organized by country and year, following the `ccyy` naming convention (e.g., `de18`, `de19`).

- In addition, each `ccyy` dataset is divided by level:

  - household-level data
  - individual-level data

Stata users have two different methods available to import data into LISSY:

1.  The newer `lissyuse` command

2.  The traditional Stata `use` command, which imports one dataset at a time, separately for household- (`h`) and person-level (`p`) data

In this guide, we focus primarily on **Method 1**, based on the `lissyuse` command. This method imports the selected LIS datasets into a single appended dataset while simplifying dataset selection and file management.

<br>

# Method 1: `lissyuse` {#import_data_stata}

The [`lissyuse`](https://www.lisdatacenter.org/wp-content/uploads/files/Job%20Submission-lissyuse.pdf) command, developed in-house and pre-installed in LISSY, provides several features designed to simplify and automate the data-import process for LIS users.

Its two main advantages are:

1.  Country series can be loaded automatically by specifying a country's iso2 and a time range.

2.  Automatically merges household- and person-level files according to the levels of the variables selected.

<br>

## Advantage 1: Importing Entire Country Series

As noted above, LIS data is stored separately for each country–year combination, identified by a `ccyy` code.

At the time of writing, LIS contains 28 datasets for Austria and 22 for Switzerland. Importing all available years manually would therefore require specifying 50 individual datasets. In addition, the user would need to know exactly which years are available for each country.

```{stata, eval =FALSE, echo=TRUE}
* at23 at22 at21 at20 at19 ...
* ch22 ch21 ch20 ch19 ch18 ...
```

To simplify this process, `lissyuse` allows users to specify only the countries they wish to work with through their ISO2 country codes, together with a desired time range using the `from()` and `to()` options:

```{stata, eval =TRUE, echo=TRUE}
lissyuse, iso2(at ch) from(2019) to(2022)
```

<br>

In this example, we specify the countries inside the `iso2()` option and restrict the selected period using the `from()` and `to()` options.

As the output shows, all relevant datasets are automatically identified, loaded, and appended into a single dataset. We can verify which datasets were imported by tabulating the variable `dname`:

```{stata, eval =TRUE, echo=TRUE, class.output = "foldable"}
quietly lissyuse, iso2(at ch) from(2019) to(2022)
tabulate dname
```

<br>

::: callout-tip
When the output of a command is not essential, consider adding `quietly` (or `qui`) before the command. This helps keep the console output and logs cleaner.
:::

You may notice a warning indicating that all variables were imported. This occurs because no variables were explicitly specified. In such cases, `lissyuse` imports all available LIS variables from both household- and person-level files.

In practice, this is usually inefficient. Therefore, users are strongly encouraged to specify variables explicitly using the `hvars()` and `pvars()` options.

<br>

## Advantage 2: Automatic Data-Level Selection

The second advantage of `lissyuse` is that it automatically determines the appropriate data level based on the variables selected.

If the user specifies only household-level variables in `hvars()`, such as disposable household income (\<b\>dhi\</b\>), household size (\<b\>nhhmem\</b\>), or geographic region (\<b\>region_c\</b\>), only household-level files are imported.

```{stata, eval =TRUE, echo=TRUE, class.output = "foldable"}
lissyuse, iso2(at ch) from(2019) to(2022) hvars(hid dname dhi nhhmem region_c hwgt) 
codebook, compact
```

In the example below, only person-level variables are specified. As a result, only person-level files are imported

```{stata, eval =TRUE, echo=TRUE, class.output = "foldable"}
quietly lissyuse, iso2(at ch) from(2019) to(2022) pvars(pid age sex relation pi11 lfs) 
codebook, compact
```

Finally, when both household-level and person-level variables are requested, `lissyuse` **automatically merges both files into a single dataset**.

```{stata, eval =TRUE, echo=TRUE, class.output = "foldable"}
quietly lissyuse, iso2(at ch) from(2019) to(2022) pvars(pid age sex relation pi11 lfs) 
codebook, compact
```

<br>

## Importing LWS data

To import LWS data, one would need to change the selection in the <b>Project</b> drop-down menu, and add the `lws` option as exemplified in the snippet below.

![](/images/project_lws.png){fig-align="center"}

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE}
quietly lissyuse, iso2(at) from(2017) to(2021) lws
codebook, compact
```

::: callout-caution
Always remeber to change the Project dropdown and to add the `lws` argument !
:::
